title: Peppermint steam inhalation for nasal congestion
subtitle: The kitchen's fastest decongestant
excerpt: Dried peppermint leaves steeped in a bowl of just-boiled water, inhaled with a towel over the head. The menthol vapour opens nasal passages and loosens mucus within minutes. One of the simplest and most effective home remedies for blocked sinuses.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), peppermint entry, menthol action on the nasal passages, steam inhalation for catarrh. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), peppermint as a nasal decongestant. EMA HMPC monograph on Menthae piperitae herba (2020), confirms menthol as the active decongestant constituent.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried peppermint leaves keep 12 months in a sealed tin. Use fresh water for each inhalation session.

recipeTools.notes: Or any large heatproof bowl, the vessel you lean over.

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary volatile constituent of peppermint, responsible for the cooling sensation and nasal decongestant effect on the mucous membranes.

content.text: Peppermint leaves in a bowl of just-boiled water, a towel over the head, five minutes breathing the steam. The

vapour hits the nasal passages immediately, creating a cooling, opening sensation and thinning the mucus that is blocking the sinuses. This is one of the few home remedies where the effect is almost immediate and virtually everyone who tries it notices the difference. Maud Grieve records steam inhalation for catarrh; the EMA's 2020 monograph confirms menthol as the active decongestant constituent of peppermint. The preparation takes about three minutes and needs nothing more than dried peppermint, hot water, a bowl, and a towel.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Children: do not use steam inhalation with any menthol preparation for children under six. Menthol can cause reflex airway closure (laryngospasm) in young children, a serious reaction. For children aged six to twelve, a very brief session (two to three minutes) with adult supervision is the traditional upper limit. Never leave a child unsupervised near a bowl of hot water.

Epilepsy: a small number of case reports link menthol inhalation to seizure threshold changes. If you have epilepsy, discuss with your neurologist before using concentrated menthol preparations.

Burn risk: the water must be off the boil before use, wait at least two minutes after boiling before leaning over the bowl.

Asthma: steam inhalation can trigger bronchoconstriction in some people with asthma. If breathing becomes worse during the inhalation, stop immediately.

See your doctor for nasal congestion lasting more than 10 days without improvement, congestion with severe headache or facial pain, or sinusitis with fever.

items.prepNote: about 1 heaped teaspoon, or 2, 3 tablespoons if using whole dried leaves
freshly boiled, then allowed to stand 2, 3 minutes

content.text: You will also need a large heatproof bowl or the large saucepan, and a towel large enough to drape over your head and the bowl. The towel traps the steam, without it the menthol disperses into the room and the inhalation is much weaker.

Place {{dried-peppermint-leaves}} of dried peppermint leaves in the bottom of the large saucepan or bowl. Boil the kettle and wait two to three minutes, the water should be hot but not at a rolling boil. Pour {{water}} of the just-off-boil water over the peppermint leaves. The leaves will release their volatile oils into the steam immediately.

Sit in a chair in front of the bowl on a stable table. Lean forward over the bowl at a comfortable distance, close enough to feel the steam but not so close that the heat is uncomfortable on the face. Drape the towel over your head and the bowl to create a tent. Breathe steadily through the nose for five to ten minutes. If the steam feels too hot, raise your head slightly or lift the edge of the towel.

One session of five to ten minutes, up to twice daily for adults, morning and evening during an acute blocked episode. The menthol effect lasts one to two hours after each session. For children aged six to twelve, one brief session (two to three minutes) with adult supervision, once daily, is the traditional upper limit.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use for children under six. Do not use if you have epilepsy without medical advice. Stop immediately if breathing becomes worse or you feel dizzy. See your doctor for congestion with severe facial pain or swelling, vision changes, fever above 38.5°C, or congestion that has not improved after 10 days.
